The central argument is that gardens should be treated as durable civic infrastructure because food access, repeated cooperation, and neighborhood resilience reinforce one another rather than operating as isolated benefits.

The food-access mechanism is proximity. A nearby plot does not replace a grocery store, but it supplements weekly ingredients and reduces some travel for households that face distance or transportation barriers.

Trust develops through repeated low-pressure work. Neighbors share tools, divide watering schedules, solve small disagreements, and gradually learn who follows through before an emergency requires coordination.

Resilience is more than the harvest because the garden creates an organizing network. People already know how to exchange information, coordinate supplies, and check on neighbors when ordinary systems are disrupted.

The storm example shows that an existing volunteer network could organize quickly. The garden did not replace delayed deliveries; it supplied a familiar place and relationships through which residents coordinated help.
The housing objection matters, so the proposal limits gardens to vacant or unsuitable parcels instead of treating every possible housing site as interchangeable with open space.
A three-year lease gives volunteers enough certainty to invest in soil and shared infrastructure, while annual reporting still lets the city reclaim a neglected site for another public need.
The strongest limitation is that gardens supplement food access but cannot solve poverty or replace grocery stores. The claim is about layered civic capacity, not a single complete solution.
